hypothesis: Tighter OBV lookback and more extreme MFI thresholds focus the strategy on clearer over‑extension of volume flow, while a shorter ATR moving‑average filter ensures we only trade in sufficiently volatile regimes; this should increase win‑rate and Sharpe by reducing marginal entries and improving stop‑loss placement.

Combining OBV extreme‑value reversals with a strong MFI overbought/oversold filter isolates moments when volume‑driven price moves are likely exhausted, while requiring ATR to be above its recent average ensures the market is sufficiently volatile for the mean‑reversion to be exploitable; this tighter filter should reduce whipsaws, improve win‑rate and Sharpe, and lower drawdown.

Combining OBV extreme points with MFI extreme readings filters out random volume spikes, while an ATR volatility filter ensures trades are taken only in sufficiently active markets; this reduces false entries and improves risk‑adjusted returns in a mean‑reversion regime.

In a mean‑reversion market the price tends to bounce after OBV reaches an extreme opposite to the price move; buying when OBV is at its recent low and MFI is oversold (and selling when OBV is at its recent high and MFI is overbought) captures the corrective move. Adding an ATR‑based volatility filter (trade only when ATR is above its recent average) removes low‑volatility noise that caused the previous strategy to take many losing trades, while the ATR stop‑loss/take‑profit keeps risk proportional to market volatility.

When OBV reaches an extreme relative to its recent range, price is likely to revert as volume pressure dissipates; confirming with MFI in oversold (for longs) or overbought (for shorts) zones filters out weak moves, while ATR‑based stops and targets size risk proportionally to market volatility.
